section.title: Spiritual Gifts and the Body
section.sectionRationale: The Corinthians prized showy gifts and ranked one another by them, splitting the church into the impressive and the overlooked. Paul answers with a double truth: every genuine gift comes from the one Spirit and is given for the common good, and the church is one body with many different members, each needed, arranged by God Himself. The chapter dismantles both pride and self-pity and prepares for the way of love in chapter 13.
section.thesis: There are many gifts but one Spirit, many members but one body; God gives every gift for the common good and arranges each member as He wills, so that no one may boast and no one may despair, for together the many are the one body of Christ in whom all suffer and rejoice as one.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the confession that Jesus is Lord, made only by the Spirit; the varieties of gifts given by the one Spirit for the common good; the one body of many members, all baptized in one Spirit; and the mutual care of the members, for you are the body of Christ.

units.label: Jesus is Lord, by the Spirit

verses.text: Now about {{pneumatikos|spiritual gifts}}, brothers, I do not want you to be uninformed.
You know that when you were pagans, you were influenced and led astray to mute idols.
Therefore I inform you that no one who is speaking by the Spirit of God says, “Jesus be cursed,” and no one can say, “Jesus is Lord,” except by the Holy Spirit.
There are different {{charisma|gifts}}, but the same Spirit.
There are different ministries, but the same Lord.
There are different ways of working, but the same God works all things in all people.
Now to each one the manifestation of the Spirit is given for the common good.
To one there is given through the Spirit the message of wisdom, to another the message of knowledge by the same Spirit,
to another faith by the same Spirit, to another gifts of healing by that one Spirit,
to another the working of miracles, to another prophecy, to another distinguishing between spirits, to another speaking in various tongues, and to still another the interpretation of tongues.
All these are the work of one and the same Spirit, who apportions them to each one as He determines.

units.label: One body, many members
units.note: God arranges each part as He wills

verses.text: The {{soma|body}} is a unit, though it is composed of many parts. And although its parts are many, they all form one body. So it is with Christ.
For in one Spirit we were all {{baptizo|baptized}} into one body, whether Jews or Greeks, slave or free, and we were all given one Spirit to drink.
For the body does not consist of one part, but of many.
If the foot should say, “Because I am not a hand, I do not belong to the body,” that would not make it any less a part of the body.
And if the ear should say, “Because I am not an eye, I do not belong to the body,” that would not make it any less a part of the body.
If the whole body were an eye, where would the sense of hearing be? If the whole body were an ear, where would the sense of smell be?
But in fact, God has arranged the members of the body, every one of them, according to His design.
If they were all one part, where would the body be?
As it is, there are many parts, but one body.

units.label: Members caring for one another
units.note: you are the body of Christ

verses.text: The eye cannot say to the hand, “I do not need you.” Nor can the head say to the feet, “I do not need you.”
On the contrary, the parts of the body that seem to be weaker are indispensable,
and the parts we consider less honorable, we treat with greater honor. And our unpresentable parts are treated with special modesty,
whereas our presentable parts have no such need. But God has composed the body and has given greater honor to the parts that lacked it,
so that there should be no division in the body, but that its members should have mutual concern for one another.
If one part suffers, every part suffers with it; if one part is honored, every part rejoices with it.
Now you are the body of Christ, and each of you is a member of it.
And in the church God has appointed first of all apostles, second prophets, third teachers, then workers of miracles, and those with gifts of healing, helping, administration, and various tongues.
Are all apostles? Are all prophets? Are all teachers? Do all work miracles?
Do all have gifts of healing? Do all speak in tongues? Do all interpret?
But eagerly desire the greater gifts. And now I will show you the most excellent way.

pneumatikos.gloss: Spiritual things or gifts, that which belongs to and comes from the Holy Spirit. Paul opens the whole discussion with this word, correcting the Corinthians' fascination with the spectacular by anchoring everything spiritual in the Spirit who exalts Jesus as Lord and works for the good of the whole church.

charisma.gloss: A gift of grace, a concrete endowment freely given by God. The word is built on charis, grace, so every gift is unearned and given to serve, not to boast in. There are different charismata but the same Spirit, so variety is His design and no gift ranks a believer above another.

soma.gloss: Body. Paul's master image for the church: one body composed of many different members, each needed, arranged by God. Just as a body is not a single organ but many working together, so the church is diverse by design, and the members belong to one another as one body of Christ.

baptizo.gloss: To baptize, immerse, or plunge. In one Spirit we were all baptized into one body: the same Spirit who unites us to Christ joins every believer, Jew or Greek, slave or free, into the single body. The verb marks entry into the one body and grounds the equality and unity of all its members.

basic.context: The Corinthians treasured the more visible gifts, especially tongues, and used them to rank one another, which only deepened their divisions. Paul redirects them. First, the mark of the Spirit is not spectacle but the confession that Jesus is Lord. Then he shows that the many gifts all come from the one Spirit and are given for the common good, and that the church is one body made of many different members, every one arranged and needed by God. Pride and self-pity both dissolve before this truth.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul is teaching the church how to think about gifts, not simply describing Corinth. His body image is an argument: because the church is one body, diversity is essential and no member is dispensable. He ends by pointing beyond the gifts to the most excellent way, love, which chapter 13 unfolds, so gifts must always be read in the light of love.
basic.originalLanguages: The topic is the pneumatikos, the things of the Spirit. The endowments are charismata, gifts of grace built on the word for grace, so they are given, not earned, and given to serve. Paul's governing picture is the soma, the body: one body of many members. And all are baptizo, baptized, in one Spirit into that one body, so unity and diversity are both the Spirit's design.
basic.typology: Israel was one people made of twelve tribes with different roles; the church is the one body of Christ made of many members with different gifts. As the tabernacle needed many crafts and callings to function, so the body of Christ needs every member. The pattern of one people composed of diverse parts, united by God's presence, finds its fulfillment in the Spirit-indwelt church.
basic.christ: The gifts exist to serve Christ and His body. The Spirit's whole aim is to lead people to confess Jesus is Lord, and the church is not merely like a body but is the body of Christ, so that what is done to the least member is done to Him. Christ is the head who unites and directs the members, and every gift finds its meaning in building up His body.
basic.god: The one God, Father, Son, and Spirit, stands behind the whole chapter: the same Spirit, the same Lord, the same God who works all in all. God is the giver who apportions gifts as He determines and the composer who arranges every member as He wills. He honors the parts that lack honor, so that the church reflects His own care for the weak and lowly.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 12 gives a parallel picture of one body with many members and differing gifts. Ephesians 4 shows Christ giving gifts to equip the saints and build up His body. 1 Corinthians 8 names the one God the Father and the one Lord Jesus Christ. 1 Corinthians 13 supplies the most excellent way that crowns the gifts.
basic.oneStory: God has always been forming one people out of many, and now by His Spirit He unites Jew and Greek, slave and free, into the one body of Christ. The variety of gifts is not a threat to unity but its very expression, a foretaste of the new creation where a countless, diverse people worships the Lamb as one. The chapter teaches the church to live now as the one body it truly is.

context.literary: Chapters 12 to 14 form one sustained argument about spiritual gifts in the assembly. Chapter 12 lays the foundation, the one Spirit and the one body; chapter 13 sets love as the indispensable way; chapter 14 applies it to orderly worship. Within chapter 12, Paul moves from the test of true spirituality, to the source and purpose of gifts, to the body image, to mutual care and a list of gifts, ending by pointing ahead to love.
context.historical: In a Greco-Roman world enamored of ecstatic religion and status, the Corinthians treated tongues as a badge of superiority and looked down on those who lacked it. Their culture ranked people by honor; Paul overturns this by insisting that the weaker and less honorable members are indispensable and are given greater honor by God, subverting the honor codes of the day.
context.canonical: The body of Christ is one of the New Testament's central images for the church, developed here and in Romans 12 and Ephesians 4. Together they teach that the Spirit distributes varied gifts to a unified body under Christ its head, forming the pattern for church life, and this chapter is its fullest single treatment.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To correct the Corinthians' prideful ranking of gifts by teaching that all gifts come from the one Spirit for the common good, and that the church is one body of many indispensable members arranged by God, so that unity and diversity together, expressed in love, mark the Spirit's true work.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul's teaching on the one Spirit, the one body, and mutual care is prescriptive truth for every church. The particular lists of gifts are illustrative rather than exhaustive, and the ordering, first apostles, second prophets, and so on, indicates function and foundation, not a scale of personal worth.

debates.question: Do the miraculous sign gifts, such as tongues, prophecy, and healing, continue today?

views.label: Continuationist
views.case: Scripture nowhere clearly withdraws these gifts before Christ returns, and the Spirit still apportions them as He determines for the church's edification; they should be welcomed and tested by Scripture.

views.label: Cessationist
views.case: The revelatory and confirmatory gifts served to found and authenticate the apostolic message, and with the completion of that foundation and the canon their normative use has ceased, while God remains free to heal in answer to prayer.

debates.bearing: Both positions confess the same Spirit as sovereign giver and both submit every claimed gift to Scripture and to love; the disagreement is over which gifts continue, not over the Spirit's authority or the primacy of love.

study.originalLanguages: Paul frames the whole subject as the pneumatikos, the things of the Spirit, refusing to let spirituality be measured by spectacle. The endowments are charismata, gifts of grace, rooted in the word charis; because they are given, no one may boast, and because they are grace they are meant to serve. His controlling image is the soma, the body, one body of many members, each necessary and arranged by God. And the ground of it all is that in one Spirit we were all baptizo, baptized, into that one body, so that Jew and Greek, slave and free, are made genuinely one, with unity and diversity alike the Spirit's design.

typology.fulfillment: As Israel was one nation of twelve tribes with distinct roles, so the church is the one body of Christ of many members with distinct gifts, now uniting Jew and Greek, slave and free.
As the tabernacle needed varied crafts and ministries to function under God's presence, so the body of Christ needs every gifted member, indwelt and arranged by the Spirit.

study.christ: Though this chapter foregrounds the Spirit, it is thoroughly centered on Christ. The Spirit's aim is to bring people to confess Jesus is Lord, the earliest Christian creed and the true test of spirituality. The church is not merely compared to a body but declared to be the body of Christ, so that to honor or wound the least member is to honor or wound Him. Christ is the head who unites and orders the members, and every gift is given to build up His body until it reflects Him. The most excellent way to which Paul points, love, is finally the love of Christ Himself lived out among His people.
study.god: The chapter is quietly Trinitarian: the same Spirit, the same Lord, the same God who works all things in all. God is the sovereign giver who apportions gifts to each one as He determines, and the wise composer who arranges every member of the body according to His design. Strikingly, He gives greater honor to the parts that lacked it, so that the church mirrors His own tender care for the weak and overlooked. We meet a God who delights in unity through diversity and who dignifies the lowly.

crossReferences.note: One body with many members and differing gifts given by grace, a close parallel to this chapter's teaching.
The ascended Christ gives gifts to equip the saints and build up His body until it reaches maturity in Him.
One God the Father and one Lord Jesus Christ, matching the same Spirit, Lord, and God of verses 4 to 6.
The most excellent way promised in verse 31, showing that gifts without love are nothing.
Baptized into Christ, illuminating being baptized in one Spirit into one body.

study.oneStory: God has always been gathering one people out of many, and by His Spirit He now unites Jew and Greek, slave and free, into the one body of Christ. The variety of gifts does not fracture that unity but expresses it, a present sign of the new creation where a vast and diverse people will worship the Lamb as one. This chapter trains the church to live now as the one body it already is in Christ, honoring the weak and building one another up in love.

quiz.q: According to Paul, what is the true test that someone is speaking by the Spirit?

options.text: That they confess Jesus is Lord, which no one can say except by the Holy Spirit.
options.note: Verse 3 makes the lordship of Jesus the mark of the Spirit's work.

options.text: That they display the more spectacular gifts like tongues.
options.note: Spectacle is not the test; Corinth's error was to think so.

options.text: That they feel a strong emotional experience.
options.note: Feelings are not the measure; confessing Jesus as Lord is.

options.text: That they were formerly devoted pagans.
options.note: Their pagan past was precisely what led them astray to mute idols.

quiz.q: Why are there different gifts in the church?

options.text: The one Spirit apportions them as He determines, for the common good.
options.note: Verses 7 and 11: given for the common good by the sovereign Spirit.

options.text: So that the more gifted believers can lead the rest.
options.note: Gifts serve the whole, not personal rank; that was Corinth's mistake.

options.text: Because believers earn different gifts by their devotion.
options.note: They are charismata, gifts of grace, not earned rewards.

options.text: So that each person can pursue his own spiritual benefit.
options.note: They are given for the common good, not private advantage.

quiz.q: What does Paul's image of the body teach about the members?

options.text: There are many different members, each needed and arranged by God, forming one body.
options.note: Verses 18 to 20: God arranges every part; the body is many yet one.

options.text: The stronger members can do without the weaker ones.
options.note: Verse 22 says the weaker parts are indispensable.

options.text: Every member should have the same gift for true unity.
options.note: If all were one part, there would be no body; diversity is the design.

options.text: Members who lack the showy gifts do not really belong.
options.note: The foot and ear still belong; no one is excluded for lacking a gift.

quiz.q: How are believers joined into the one body?

options.text: In one Spirit they were all baptized into one body, whether Jew or Greek, slave or free.
options.note: Verse 13 grounds the unity of the body in the one Spirit.

options.text: By achieving the same level of spiritual maturity.
options.note: Unity rests on the Spirit's work, not on equal maturity.

options.text: By belonging to the same social class or nation.
options.note: Paul deliberately unites Jew and Greek, slave and free.

options.text: By each keeping to their own separate group.
options.note: The Spirit makes the many one body, not many separate groups.

quiz.q: What happens in the body when one member suffers or is honored?

options.text: If one suffers, all suffer; if one is honored, all rejoice together.
options.note: Verse 26 describes the mutual concern of the members.

options.text: The stronger members carry on unaffected.
options.note: That contradicts mutual concern; the whole body is affected.

options.text: Only the leaders need to respond to it.
options.note: Every member shares in the suffering and the joy, not just leaders.

options.text: Nothing, since each member is independent.
options.note: Verse 21 denies that any member can say I do not need you.

questions: Paul says the mark of the Spirit is confessing Jesus is Lord. How does that reframe what you count as evidence of the Spirit at work in someone's life?
Every gift is given for the common good. What gift has God given you, and how are you using it to build up others rather than yourself?
The foot and ear were tempted to feel they did not belong. Where are you tempted to disqualify yourself, or to envy someone else's gift, instead of trusting God's arrangement?
God gives greater honor to the parts that lacked it. Who are the overlooked, weaker members of your church, and how could you honor them this week?
If one part suffers, all suffer. How closely do you actually share in the pain and joy of others in your congregation, and what would deeper mutual concern look like?

translationNotes.note: Spiritual gifts renders the plural pneumatikos, literally the spiritual things; the term can include both the gifts and the people who are spiritual, but here it introduces the whole discussion of Spirit-given endowments.
Gifts translates charismata, gifts of grace built on the word charis; the term stresses that every endowment is freely given by God to serve, never earned or a ground for boasting.
Baptized in one Spirit into one body uses baptizo; the emphasis falls on the Spirit uniting all believers, across every human division, into the single body of Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 12:1-31; any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas, with wording unchanged.
